front: Empire: built on an artificial island (or islands) in Lake Texcoco, this was the capital of what empire?
Event: this city became the site of a modern capital after the Spanish conquest
Empire: one source described this city as "as large as Seville or Cordova," with further information found in what codex?
Empire: the "Bancroft dialogues" challenge the prevailing theory about what aspect of this city's name?
Event: this city's founders settled where they saw an eagle eating a snake while perched atop a cactus
Event: this city's founders had been exiled from Culhuacan after sacrificing the local leader's daughter and wearing her flayed skin
Institution: this city was divided into four zones (campans), each split into about 20 calpulli districts, together housing over 200,000 people
Institution: this city's agriculture relied on "floating gardens" created by fencing off sections of the lakebed
Institution: this city was supplied with fresh water by a dual-pipe clay (later stone) aqueduct fed by springs at "grasshopper hill"
Institution: a ten-mile dike associated with an allied ruler of Texcoco separated this city's lake into saltwater and freshwater sections
Institution: a daily crew of about 1,000 people swept this city's streets, collecting biodegradable trash that was incinerated for lighting
Institution: canoes in this city collected human urine (used as a fabric-dye mordant) and excrement (used as fertilizer) in a reportedly zero-waste system
Institution: chinampa structures on which this city was built are still visible today in a neighboring lake
Institution: boys and girls in this city were educated separately, at "Houses of Youth" and "Houses of Song" respectively
Institution: this city's Sacred Precinct held a dwelling for the Eagle Warriors and a huge skull rack estimated to hold about 60,000 sacrificial skulls
work: Templo Mayor and the Sacred Precinct

front: Institution: this city's central pyramid bore twin shrines to the rain god Tlaloc and the war god Huitzilopochtli, the "left-handed hummingbird"
work: Templo Mayor and the Sacred Precinct

front: Event: excavation at this city resumed in 1978 after utility workers discovered an 8.5-ton stone disc depicting a dismembered moon goddess
work: Templo Mayor and the Sacred Precinct

front: Institution: an artifact possibly representing the southern stars via a goddess's marigold headdress was found in a structure in this city emulating the mythological "Serpent Mountain"
work: Templo Mayor and the Sacred Precinct

front: Institution: a blue water pot found in this city was dedicated to a rain god whose first wife was kidnapped by a night god before he married a jade-skirted goddess
work: Templo Mayor and the Sacred Precinct

front: Empire: this city's ruler Itzcoatl organized a triple alliance with Texcoco and Tlacopan against what rival Tepanec city?
work: The Triple Alliance and Rise to Power

front: Empire: after the Triple Alliance's defeat of Azcapotzalco, this city became the most powerful in the Valley of Mexico
work: The Triple Alliance and Rise to Power

front: Ruler: one ruler of this city took his name from a mythical man-eating dog said to live underwater
Ruler: this ruler added the House of the Eagle Warriors to his rebuilding of this city's Templo Mayor
Ruler: this city's best-known ruler kept two palace zoos -- one for birds of prey, one for other animals -- together employing over 300 people
Ruler: a post-conquest indigenous "judge-governor" of this city, nicknamed "mushroom" after losing a court case over a head tax, was badly injured falling off a roof during a mock sword fight
Ruler: that same "mushroom"-nicknamed judge-governor commissioned a featherwork rendition of what artwork as a gift for Pope Paul III?
Ruler: the second post-conquest "judge-governor" of this city presided over a hemorrhagic "great pestilence" identified by a 2018 study as what disease?
Ruler: this city's last independent tlatoani was eventually hanged from a ceiba tree in Acalan
Ruler: the treasure of this ruler was captured by the pirate Jean Fleury after his execution
Event: Pedro de Alvarado's troops massacred noblemen and warriors at this city's Great Temple during a religious festival
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the City

front: Event: foreign accounts claim a ruler of this city was stoned to death by his own subjects, prompting the occupying army to flee via its western causeway
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the City

front: Event: this city fell after a siege of how many days by Hernan Cortes?
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the City

front: Figure: this conquistador, who claimed to have fought in over 130 battles in Mexico including the final siege of this city, wrote the "True History of the Conquest of New Spain"
work: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the City

front: Event: goods from this city "rejoiced [the] heart" of what German artist when a royal tour brought them to Brussels?
